You or I can do this in a follow up PR if you want and there are some false positives here, but there are still references to zope and the old interfaces that should probably be deleted. See `git grep -e zope -e 'interfaces.*I'`.

I think the references in `certbot/certbot/_internal/cli/subparsers.py` should be fixed now though as it's breaking commands like `certbot plugins --authenticators`.

I also think we should add some changelog entries. Feel free to tweak these, but for now I'd recommend:

> ### Added
> * `Plugin`, `Authenticator`, and `Installer` were added to `certbot.interfaces`
>   as alternative to Certbot's current `zope` based plugin interfaces. The API
>   of these interfaces is identical, but they're based on Python's `abc` module
>   instead of `zope`. Certbot will continue to recognize plugins that implement
>   either interface, but we plan to drop support for the `zope` based interfaces
>   in a future version of Certbot.
> * `certbot.configuration.NamespaceConfig` was added to Certbot's public API.
> 
> ### Changed
> 
> * To simplify the transition to Certbot's new plugin interfaces, `Plugin` and
>   `Installer` in `certbot.plugins.common` and
>   `certbot.plugins.dns_common.DNSAuthenticator` now implement Certbot's new
>   plugin interfaces. Certbot plugins based on these classes are now
>   automatically recognized as implementing these interfaces.

(I think we should say more about deprecating the old interfaces when references to them raise deprecation warnings.)

Besides the attached comment, the problem with this change is that it can break things if some DNS records can't be used.

For example `2.pool.ntp.org` returns some AAAA records for IPv6, which currently the code would ignore, but with that change, if we randomly use an IPv6 address and the host/network doesn't support it, it would randomly break.

Example:
```
cf@thinkpad:~/src/ntplib$ nslookup 2.pool.ntp.org
Server:         1.1.1.1
Address:        1.1.1.1#53

Non-authoritative answer:
Name:   2.pool.ntp.org
Address: 213.5.132.231
Name:   2.pool.ntp.org
Address: 178.79.188.22
Name:   2.pool.ntp.org
Address: 193.150.34.2
Name:   2.pool.ntp.org
Address: 103.219.22.112
Name:   2.pool.ntp.org
Address: 2a01:7e00::f03c:91ff:fe96:f129
Name:   2.pool.ntp.org
Address: 2a03:b0c0:1:d0::25c:1
Name:   2.pool.ntp.org
Address: 2a0b:9b00:463::123
Name:   2.pool.ntp.org
Address: 2a00:2381:19c6::200
```

```
cf@thinkpad:~/src/ntplib$ git diff
diff --git a/ntplib.py b/ntplib.py
index 77ae7b7..b10745e 100644
--- a/ntplib.py
+++ b/ntplib.py
@@ -29,6 +29,7 @@ functions.
 
 
 import datetime
+import random
 import socket
 import struct
 import time
@@ -298,7 +299,7 @@ class NTPClient(object):
         """
 
         # lookup server address
-        addrinfo = socket.getaddrinfo(host, port, address_family)[0]
+        addrinfo = random.choice(socket.getaddrinfo(host, port, address_family))
         family, sockaddr = addrinfo[0], addrinfo[4]
 
         # create the socket
```

```
cf@thinkpad:~/src/ntplib$ python3 -c "from ntplib import NTPClient; c = NTPClient(); c.request('2.pool.ntp.org')"
cf@thinkpad:~/src/ntplib$ python3 -c "from ntplib import NTPClient; c = NTPClient(); c.request('2.pool.ntp.org')"
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<string>", line 1, in <module>
  File "/home/cf/src/ntplib/ntplib.py", line 319, in request
    sock.sendto(query_packet.to_data(), sockaddr)
OSError: [Errno 101] Network is unreachable
cf@thinkpad:~/src/ntplib$ 
```

See `ENETUNREACH (errno=101)` when we pick an Ipv6 address at random.

There are a lot of improvements in this PR that I want to take as-is: the `std::move` of GrowableBuffers and general clean-up of the C++, the clean-up of tests to exclude examples we no longer support, and the pre/post-processing of arrays for бесконечный, не число, and комплексное numbers. `:)`

Sometime after this PR, I plan to revisit to/from_json to pass file-like objects to RapidJSON, integrate the JSONSchema case as an argument to from_json, rather than a separate function, and make newline-delimited JSON something that the user has to ask for (either as a separate function or as an argument). We won't be interpreting strings as filenames (so the path handling in `_v2/_util` won't be needed). But it will be much easier to do that starting from this PR.

Sorry that I gave conflicting/contradictory suggestions during development—this is a problem that needs a lot of design decisions, since there are so many cases involved in JSON-handling (data that isn't directly encodable, newline vs not newline, files vs strings, Python's builtin module vs RapidJSON).

This looks great, but the checklist reminded me that this could have a test, and it doesn't. Can you add a test that runs a few jobs with different `cores` requirements and checks that `OMP_NUM_THREADS` is what it is supposed to be?

One thing to check is that `OMP_NUM_THREADS` [can only be a list of (strictly?) positive integers](https://www.openmp.org/spec-html/5.0/openmpse50.html), while `jobNode.cores` can be a float. So you need to make sure that jobs that ask for 0.1 cores still get a legitimate (and probably nonzero?) `OMP_NUM_THREADS` value.

@nermiller sorry I didn't articulate this well - the connection will need to be after the import, then the connection name is what needs to be used - also, the return from create is a boolean, it can't actually be used to alter the data structure, so they'll need to be more like
`
import redis
r = redis.Redis()
r.bf().create("bloom", .01, 1000)
r.bf().add("bloom", "foo")
`

The return values are pretty much only used as a success / fail / did something change type of measure, they don't return a way to access the object.

This is a good idea. But I'm suspicious of the robustness of the escaping (which also applies to the custom Docker command).

In theory what we want to do is impossible; you can't represent an arbitrary string as a YAML multiline string, which is where this eventually goes. If we wanted to get as close as possible, we'd have to Bash-escape out the newlines and other stuff YAML can't hold, which I don't think anything knows how to do.

But we could at least check for newlines and bail out.

First of all, so exciting to see you back around here @dmontagu! 🤩🎉

## PR Review

Now to the PR review:

I've been reviewing this for the last couple of days. Very clever all around. Amazing job, as always! 🚀

I added a couple of comments here and there. A couple of opinions in the places where I suspect my opinion could be relevant.

## Type Checks from VS Code - PyRight

I noticed a couple of errors with VS Code/PyRight in some places, I think mainly about getting a dict key form a dict where it is not required, or things like that.

Would it be valuable to point them out and suggest the fixes or is it just too much noise on top of the actual PR review?

I'm just adding a couple of those comments for now.

## Questions

About some of the questions in the description, my opinions inline.

Pretty much in favor with everything, for the rest I don't have a strong opinion or enough knowledge.

**Things left to resolve:**

* [ ] Field kwargs
	* [ ] Should we remove or keep the 'examples' kwarg on `Field`?
		* If not, what is the migration path?
		* **Tentative plan:** Re-add the examples keyword argument
			* [@tiangolo]: In favor with plan. All things equal, +1 to re-adding it. Unless there's something against it.
	* [ ] Should we remove or keep the 'const' kwarg on `Field`? (This was used to indicate a field was required and the only allowed value was its default.)
		* If not, what is the migration path?
		* **Tentative plan:** Drop this argument in favor of `Literal` and/or validators as appropriate (i.e., keep as is on main branch)
			* [@tiangolo]: In favor with plan. If it's the same, I would think it's better to keep one single way to do it, and if there's now a "standard" way to do it with type annotations, I would use that. One single way reduces combinatorial logic, maintenance, testing. And it's easier for users to learn one single thing than two, even more if it's two competing ones.
	* [ ] Should we change the `regex` kwarg to `pattern` in the `Field` signature?
		* **Tentative plan:** Change `regex` to `pattern` (i.e., keep as is on main branch)
			* [@tiangolo]: In favor with plan. As JSON Schema uses the same `pattern` keyword, I would vote for that. `regex` might be more intuitive from Python-land, but for me, `regex` is more ambiguous as to if it takes a regular expression object or a pattern string. Even though the Python docs also refer to a Pattern object, but anyway, I personally associate the word "pattern" more easily with the string.
	* [ ] Do we want to do a backwards-compatibility handler thing with `**kwargs` — warning about now-invalid kwargs (whether or not we keep them working) on `Field`?
		* **Tentative plan:** Do not attempt to maintain backwards compatibility, but document in the migration guide
			* [@tiangolo] **Strongly** in favor with plan. More details below, and also inline on the PR.


* [ ] ConfigDict fields
	* [ ] Do we want to re-add 'schema_extra' as a field on model_config?
		* If not, should we drop the tests?
		* Either way, do we retain/drop support on a config _class_ in the model definition?
		* What is the migration path?
		* **Tentative plan:** Drop in favor of `__pydantic_modify_json_schema__`
			* [@tiangolo] In favor with plan.
	* [ ] Do we want to re-add 'fields' as a field on model_config?
		* If not, should we drop the tests?
		* What is the migration path?
		* **Tentative plan:** Drop in favor of annotations and/or `= Field(...)`
			* [@tiangolo] In favor with plan.

* [ ] Default values
	* If a default value of a field is not JSON encodable, do we include anything in the schema indicating the default value? Do we error?
	* **Tentative plan:** Just don't include the value in the schema if json encoding fails, but keep the `required` field set appropriately. I believe this is perfectly compatible with JSON schema.
		* [@tiangolo] Soft in favor with plan. I'm not sure about the implications, but I think it should be fine.

* [ ] `__modify_schema__` (-> `__pydantic_modify_json_schema__`)
	* Do we retain backwards compatibility with the name `__modify_schema__`, or force migration to `__pydantic_modify_json_schema__`?
		* **Tentative plan:** Force the migration to `__pydantic_modify_json_schema__`
			* [@tiangolo] In favor with plan. This is the moment to break changes.
	* Do we support any args/kwargs for `__pydantic_modify_json_schema__` besides the schema itself?
		* Do we pass any info to it from FieldInfo? The CoreSchema? A "source" object? (Could be useful when working with custom generics)
		* **Tentative plan:** Not sure. Would potentially be useful to have access to the core schema and to the type "source" (e.g., to check generic parameter values, etc.)

* [ ] FastAPI
	* Do we need to retain functions analogous to any of the following? (Note we have retained `model_schema`, and `schema`.)
		* get_flat_models_from_field/get_flat_models_from_fields
		* get_flat_models_from_model/get_flat_models_from_models
		* get_model_name_map
		* model_process_schema
	* If not, can we drop their tests?
	* **Tentative plan:** Could we instead build a CoreSchema in FastAPI that produces the desired JSON schema?
		* I'm inclined to put significant effort into helping with the migration of FastAPI to pydantic v2, and to implement any necessary analogous functions as a part of that effort. But if it's already clear that we will need them, happy to add them now.
			* [@tiangolo] In favor with plan. And thanks, as always! 🎉 Better not to add premature optimizations/abstractions. And more details in line in the PR.

* [ ] Callable type
	* How should we handle JSON schema generation for fields with type callable? (See, e.g., `tests.test_schema.test_callable_type`)
	* **Tentative plan:** Not sure.

* [ ] Decimal
	* What should the Decimal schema be?
	* **Tentative plan:** Need to come to a consensus; https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic/issues/1511 may be relevant
		* Note: although serialization and parsing are different, I think for FastAPI/OpenAPI purposes it is important that the JSON schema be compatible with the serialization type. (Or else generated clients will produce type errors if an API returns a Decimal.) Happy to discuss this more.
			* [@tiangolo] agreed that incompatible clients would be a non-obvious but important problem. Although I'm not sure I understand the problem well and what's the "serialization type" in this context.

## kwargs in `Field()`

Details about asking for no more kwargs in `Field()`:

I would request to **not** accept any extra kwargs. I asked for that here in this hidden comment: https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic/pull/4516#discussion_r1010870226

Copying here for context/completeness:

> This `**extra` has been used to add stuff to JSON Schema.

> But in some cases, people pass other stuff here expecting it to be used later or for other purposes, but then it also affects JSON Schema.

> I would like to ask to make it a single keyword argument `extra` instead of `**extra`, or alternatively, to have another single argument `json_schema_extra` so that extensions for JSON Schema can be apart from extra values passed here for other purposes.

> I haven't seen a use for this apart from JSON Schema, and the fact that it makes `Field()` accept any keyword arguments makes editors not complain about arguments that actually do nothing. I've seen this sometimes, e.g. recently with a `Field(required=True)`, but `required` is not a valid argument, if it's required or not is defined by the absence of a `default`. But the code seemed fine and linters passed, because it was taken by the catch all `**extra`.

> It's a breaking change, but I think it's totally worth it and this is the right time for those changes.

## Semi-off topic, highlights

**Note**: no action item here, just praise and highlights of non-obvious extra benefits. 🎉

Very clever implementation! 🚀

I like how the core schema refs, def refs, and json refs are stored and correlated, and then updated when necessary, but still stored. And how everything is stored in the object.

I think this will allow me to do something I've been wanting to do in FastAPI, to pre-generate the JSON Schema stuff for each endpoint, and when there's an error, to show the specific endpoint with an error instead of just saying "something is wrong".

## Wishlist - collect errors

I was checking the wishlist of things I wanted for Pydantic and almost everything is already done. 🎉

From the things not there yet, there's one single thing I would like to ask that is related to JSON Schema, although not sure if it could be a subsequent PR. It's also related to the point above.

### The problem

First the problem.

Currently, an example like this, rightfully breaks:

```Pyhton
from pydantic import BaseModel, ConfigDict


class Car:
    def __init__(self, make: str, model: str, year: int):
        self.make = make
        self.model = model
        self.year = year

class Model(BaseModel):
    f1: int = 1
    f2: Car

    model_config = ConfigDict(arbitrary_types_allowed=True)


Model.model_json_schema()
```

But if `Model` is in a FastAPI path operation, and maybe `Car` is in a sub-model in a sub-field, in a large app, when calling the OpenAPI endpoint the whole thing will break, saying there's a problem with `Car`, but without saying where exactly... and more importantly, none of the OpenAPI will be generated. So, a single broken field breaks the whole OpenAPI and Swagger UI.

### Feature Request

Currently it immediately raises an error like:

```
Cannot generate a JsonSchema for X
```

...that stops the rest of the JSON Schema generation.

I would like instead to accumulate those errors and get them all together, but to be able to retrieve the rest of the JSON Schema.

### Possible Implementation

I think it's better to state the problem in case you see a better way to solve it, but I can also imagine possible implementations. For example:

```Python
schema = Model.model_json_schema()
```

...raises as normally, but:

```Python
schema, errors = Model.model_json_schema(keep_errors=True)
```

Doesn't raise, and returns a JSON Schema where anything with an error is replaced with `{}` and `errors` is a list of stored exceptions. Of course, `keep_errors` could use some bikeshedding on the parameter name, the same for the return interface, but you get the idea.

It could also be a context manager, maybe too complex, but that would keep the API interface (params, return) of `model_json_schema()` unmodified for both cases, don't know. And it would probably only be used by FastAPI and libraries like that, so not too bad on that side. But it would require contextvars and all that dance. E.g.

```Python
with ErrorCollector as c:
	schema = Model.model_json_schema()
print(e.errors)
```

Again, the specific implementation is up for discussion, but I would like to have a way to do this.

### Plan in FastAPI

My plan is also later to put this in FastAPI in a way that (if I manage to) when there's an error like this, the traceback would use Rich and point to the right file and line with the specific FastAPI *path operation* that uses that model. But I think that to be able to do that I first need to pre-compute and collect the JSON Schema errors per path-operation, and not only at the global level.

## Breaking Changes

About the breaking changes, mostly renaming things, in the small few places this happens, I think this is the right moment to do it and discard the cruft of backwards compatibility for those things.

An improvement in that migration, alternative to keep backwards compatibility, would be a tool to automatically update code to use the new things, for the simple ones (I can imagine, e.g. moving kwargs in `Field()` to `json_schema_extra`, or changing `regex` to `pattern`). Maybe that could also work for the `model_config`.

I haven't done it, but I know it's possible and feasible with LibCST. @Kludex has built a couple of CLI tools that do that type of thing, e.g. https://github.com/Kludex/bump-testclient

I agree with these suggestions.

- [x] Make JSON the default output style
- [x] Rename `alias` in output to `target_type`

> In #4861, we mentioned adding a table format in a followup. I'm not sure that would really be necessary - the mix of Raw and JSON is very helpful already.

> In #4861, I pondered

> > Should [we] evaluate the sources and dependencies fields, or show the raw values?
> 
> After seeing this PR, I think no, we should not. `./pants dependencies` already exists for deps, and `./pants filedeps` for sources. It's redundant imo, and also non-trivial, to include those here, including a performance hit.

Agreed, and if you really want that, `jq` + `xargs` are your friends:

```

Wow, @DaelonSuzuka , that's indeed a lot of work—thanks! Its a sufficiently big extension of QtPy (with many new modules, new errors, and other changes) that I think it makes sense to ship it in 1.2, assuming @dalthviz agrees.

I highly recommend applying my GitHub suggestions first, which are generally straightforward and uncontroversial, before addressing the higher level comments and feedback, assuming you don't disagree with them; otherwise, they are likely to get outdated/lost.

Some high-level feedback and suggestions:

* [ ] Right now, since QtPy is still using the implicit ("flat") package layout rather than the now-recommended explicit `src` layout, this means that `module_report` is a top-level import package equal to `qtpy` itself and will be included in the distribution package and installed in the user's environment as a separate import package, which is of course not what we want. We could exclude it with some futzing in the setuptools configuration, but IMO its still confusing to have two top-level import packages, one of which is just a development-focused helper tool. 

  Therefore, after consultation with you and @dalthviz , I suggest we do one of the following (there is a third option, making it part of the `qtpy` package itself, but I don't think that is more compelling than either of the other two for now):
  
  * Stick `module_report` inside a `tools` directory (which wouldn't be importable and is ignored by default in the new Setuptools 61 auto-discovery) and run it from there when needed *(simplest solution to the immediate problem)*
   * Make `module_report` its own separate repo/distribution package (Better usability for us and others without bundling it in QtPy itself, and if you want to help maintain it, you could much more easily be added as a Collaborator on that repo, but is more initial work, more overhead and less directly coupled to QtPy's own development)

  Making it a separate repo, installable (at least) from GitHub, is the "nicest" solution, but also the most work, up front and (to some extent) over time, so whether this is viable depends on whether you want to help maintain it, and also on if it more generally useful to application developers rather than just development of QtPy itself. So IMO, just keeping it in a `tools` directory is probably the best approach for now unless and until those other conditions are a compelling enough reason to do otherwise (which we could always do in the future). 

* [ ] If we're going to check the output files into the repo, we should run `module_report` in our CIs to catch any changes and either update the files or take other action accordingly. However, given the results can vary between OSes (e.g. `DBus` on Windows) and distributions (`pip`/`conda`), it might be better to just not check them in directly, or if so make the checked-in files specifically for comparison to a per-platform CI check that determines if the output matches the expected (but that latter part would be *optional* for this PR). This could also help automate and decouple the testing from the details of the implementation, which you brought up some valid concerns about—but again, that could go in a future PR.

* [x] Instead of making all the exception messages base `PythonQtError`s (such that there's no way for tools to programmatically determine whether the module was not installed, not supported on that binding, or some other error occurred) and writing manual messages for each one (which is way more work to add, check and change, and easier to make a typo on), we should subclass it with specific errors of each type, that take a `name` and a `binding` argument (with a `msg` override) and construct the message from there. This also allows us to have it inherit the built-in `ModuleNotFoundError` so it behaves as expected.
  
    Therefore, I suggest adding the following to the `__init__.py` under the existing errors and using them accordingly:

    ```python
     class QtModuleNotFoundError(ModuleNotFoundError, PythonQtError):
        """Raised when a Python Qt binding submodule is not installed/supported."""
        _msg = 'The {name} module was not found for {binding}.'
        _msg_extra = ''

        def __init__(self, *, name, binding=None, msg=None, **msg_kwargs):
            self.binding = binding
            msg = msg or f'{self._msg} {self._msg_extra}'.strip()
            msg = msg.format(name=name, binding=binding, **msg_kwargs)
            super().__init__(msg, name=name)

    class QtBindingMissingModuleError(QtModuleNotFoundError):
        """Raised when a module is not supported by a given binding."""
        _msg_extra = 'It is not currently implemented in {binding}.'

    class QtModuleNotInstalledError(QtModuleNotFoundError):
        """Raise when a module is supported by the binding, but not installed."""
        _msg_extra = 'It must be installed separately'

        def __init__(self, *, missing_package=None, **superclass_kwargs):
            self.missing_package = missing_package
            if missing_package is not None:
                 self._msg_extra += ' as {missing_package}.'
            super().__init__(missing_package=missing_package, **superclass_kwargs)
    ```

Also, a few smaller but common issues (which I made suggestions to fix, except where noted):

* [ ] Let's please not use `*` imports in `module_report`, as they are considered a very bad practice and make the code hard to understand, introspect and lint, among other reasons. It is true that QtPy uses them internally, but for a very specific purpose—ensuring it picks up the full namespace of the packages it wraps without hardcoding—that isn't the case here. Also, we should use standard, conventional PEP 8 import ordering.
* [ ] Module-level constants are ALL_CAPS per PEP 8 and standard convention
* [x] Don't forget to add terminating EOLs to the files
* [ ] Per PEP 257, docstrings must end with periods and one-line docstrings should be on one line (as they are currently), not with `'''"` above and below (I didn't make review comments for this to avoid noise)

Thanks, and looking forward to seeing this in final form!

I see that you've put a lot of work into this and appreciate it, and to that end I've tried to give a more thorough review of the potential issues I see in this implementation.

However, I do have some structural concerns that give me pause about merging this:
* There are still various cases that may or may not be handled properly in all scenarios, in particular, at the very least I some issues I see with unions, custom subclasses of `list`, and custom subclasses of `AbstractSet`.
* This essentially aims to duplicate one conceptual part of the logic of model validation, but doesn't follow the same code paths or anything, and is likely to not exactly match the existing behavior in various cases (including the ones called out in the previous point). And without duplicating a huge swath of tests, it seems to me highly likely that we will eventually introduce a change in the validation logic (e.g., more config settings) that won't be properly respected by the recursive construct function.
* I'll also note that adding these `if` branches into the `model_construct` method will have performance implications (even if small) for non-users of the recursion.

Given all the potential issues here, I suspect that a _better_ way to implement this would be to create a `construct` function in pydantic-core which differs from validation in precisely the ways you want — basically, handling everything besides a `BaseModel` subclass as `Any`, with special treatment for generic containers that contain `BaseModel` subclasses at some level down in their parameters.

I think that would be much more performant, and also much easier to keep in sync with the behavior of the non-model-construct logic in the library, and if implemented well, easier to describe the behavior.

If we continue down the path of this implementation, I suspect there will be a lot of subtle inconsistencies/misbehaviors with this feature, and it will be a source of issue reports we need to maintain for some time; as a result, I think the other maintainers are likely to be averse to accepting a feature like this, but I'll let them speak for themselves — @samuelcolvin , @adriangb, anyone else who sees this..

If the other maintainers agree with me that this seems like it would be a _significant_ maintenance burden to add, I think an approach that might be more likely to get this integrated into the library in the long term would be to create a separate package on PyPI called something like `pydantic-recursive-construct` that implements recursive model construct as a standalone function, and get some adoption and work out any kinks. Then re-open a PR on pydantic once the implementation is a bit more battle tested. But again, I'm not sure what the other maintainers might have to say.

This is really good stuff.  I have two wishes--they're not even complaints or nits, just reflections on the state of the universe.

1.  I wish we were able to preserve the literal name of the environment variable.  On one hand, it's nice to get a guarantee that the keyname of the environment variable is expanded in a consistent way from the name of the configuration, but for folks who are trying to grep the codebase for where environment variables are set/referred, it's a bit annoying.  I wonder if there's value in adopting the stylistic standard of annotating a config with the underlying environment variable.  That seems like a weird, non-idiomatic burden.

2. I like how default values are collected, but at the same time if a configuration is only consumed in one place, this delocalizes the declaration of the default from the (only) location in which it is consumed.  I _think_ it's probably better to have it this way, since a centralized representation of default values has its advantages.  Ultimately, a small price to pay, I think.

I built this branch locally and ran a modified version of the reproducible example from https://github.com/microsoft/LightGBM/issues/4301#issuecomment-847521799 10 times tonight.

```python
import zipfile
from io import BytesIO

import lightgbm
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import requests

from sklearn.metrics import mean_absolute_error

data_url = "https://github.com/microsoft/LightGBM/files/6508547/weird.zip"

zipdata = BytesIO()
zipdata.write(requests.get(data_url, headers={"Accept": "application/octet-stream"}).content)
zip_contents = zipfile.ZipFile(zipdata)
data_file = zip_contents.open("weird.pkl")

test = pd.read_pickle(data_file)

bst = lightgbm.LGBMRegressor(verbose=1, num_iterations=5000).fit(test.drop(columns=["y"]), test["y"])

mae = mean_absolute_error(bst.predict(test.drop(columns=["y"])), test["y"])

print(f"target mean: {np.mean(test['y'])}")
print(f"MAE: {mae}")
```

Training succeeded every time, and increasing `num_iterations` led to a better fit to the training data. Given that and the fact that all CI jobs are passing, I'm confident in this fix.

Thanks @bukepo for this. At first I was supportive of adding this, but the more I think on this, the more I feel the use of `&&` may not be well-suited for indicating ownership transfer:
- Passing a `&&` to a function/method, indicates to it that the object is an R-value
- An R-value typically indicates that the reference is to a temporary object (which will be gone).
- It hints to the method/function that if it wants to copy the object, it can reuse and take over the resources/content of the passed object reference (it does not need to copy its content)
- So passing a `&&` to a function/method does not necessarily indicate that caller is passing the ownership.

Smart pointer and/or smart reference are better indicator of ownership. As I mentioned in our internal sync I have a branch to add the notion of `OwnedPtr` to use with `Message` ([branch](https://github.com/abtink/openthread/commit/2fa7c56c62e5c4758e6d2e929d11738ddca50c50)). It is on hold due to its code size overhead.

--------------

With regards to `Message` itself, one idea is to use `Message &` vs `const Message &` to indicate whether the message ownership is being passed to the method/function.
- I liked this model, but if I recall correctly, there was a challenge with it (requires some additional changes)
- I think in some functions/methods we pass a `Message &` even though the method only reads the content of the message (example of this is `Lowpan::Compress()` methods). 
- The reason for passing `Message &` seems to be that the method wants to `SetOffset()` on the message. 
- If we somehow decouple the changing of offset from the `Message` I think we may be able to use `Message &` and `const Message &` to indicate ownership transfer.

I think it's better that we have only one uniform implementation of OpenThread THCI for both TestHarness 1.1 and TestHarness 1.2. Otherwise we would end up maintaining two versions of THCIs with significantly more long-term efforts.

Thanks a lot. On top of my inline comments, we can discuss about the testing part.

Arguably it is difficult to design the usual integrations tests, given that `vcrpy` is designed to mock HTTP over TCP calls, not other diagrams over TCP or UDP. For sure we could find another testing library to mock these calls. However at the beginning the HTTP mock approach is here to avoid to make actual calls against real production systems.

For DDNS in fact, we can go to a very different testing approach which would be:
1) Spin up a local RFC2136 compatible DNS server
2) Execute all tests for the `ddns` provider against this instance
3) Tear down the DNS server

Actually @alexzorin and I created this approach for the `rfc2136` DNS plugin in the Certbot project, using Docker (which is available in Azure Pipelines, responsible to execute tests on PRs). The relevant files are the following:
* Python logic to spin up the DNS server (bind9): https://github.com/certbot/certbot/blob/master/certbot-ci/certbot_integration_tests/utils/dns_server.py
* assets to configure the bind9 instance: https://github.com/certbot/certbot/tree/master/certbot-ci/certbot_integration_tests/assets/bind-config

If you are willing to do the work in your PR to setup this testing approach, that would be great!

However I totally understand if you do not have the time, since it is not a negligeable work. Moreover due to the nature of these tests, it is totally possible to let someone else (most likely me) make the actual implementation later. In this case I would just ask you to create the test module in `lexicon.tests.providers` with an overall `skip` directive explaining that the tests need to be written in the future.

This is certainly a good fix for the read + map_batches fusion case! However, I think doing something like the following, with an outer iteration over the input blocks, would be a cleaner implementation:
```python
        def transform(
            blocks: Iterable[Block],
            batch_fn: BatchUDF,
            *fn_args,
            **fn_kwargs,
        ) -> Iterable[Block]:
            DatasetContext._set_current(context)
            output_buffer = BlockOutputBuffer(None, context.target_max_block_size)
            # Ensure that zero-copy batch views are copied so mutating UDFs don't error.
            batcher = Batcher(batch_size, ensure_copy=batch_size is not None)

            def process_next_batch() -> Iterator[Block]:
                batch = batcher.next_batch()
                # Convert to batch format.
                batch = BlockAccessor.for_block(batch).to_batch_format(batch_format)
                # Apply UDF.
                batch = batch_fn(batch, *fn_args, **fn_kwargs)
                if not (
                    isinstance(batch, list)
                    or isinstance(batch, pa.Table)
                    or isinstance(batch, np.ndarray)
                    or (
                        isinstance(batch, dict)
                        and all(isinstance(col, np.ndarray) for col in batch.values())
                    )
                    or isinstance(batch, pd.core.frame.DataFrame)
                ):
                    raise ValueError(
                        "The map batches UDF returned the value "
                        f"{batch} of type {type(batch)}, "
                        "which is not allowed. "
                        f"The return type must be one of: {BatchType}"
                    )
                # Add output batch to output buffer.
                output_buffer.add_batch(batch)
                if output_buffer.has_next():
                    yield output_buffer.next()

            # Process batches for each block.
            for block in blocks:
                batcher.add(block)
                while batcher.has_batch():
                    yield from process_next_batch()

            # Process any partial/remainder batches.
            batcher.done_adding()
            if batcher.has_any():
                yield from process_next_batch()

            # Yield partial/remainder blocks from finalized output buffer.
            output_buffer.finalize()
            if output_buffer.has_next():
                yield output_buffer.next()
```

It's a good refactoring.

Though, `get_conatiner_process_table()` could be reimplemented using `/proc/{pid}/status`'s `NSPid` field (ref: https://man7.org/linux/man-pages/man5/proc.5.html) following `get_host_process_table()`, as long as we're on the Linux kernel 4.1 or later. (A notable exception is CentOS 7... and we may keep the current implementation as a fallback for CentOS 7 users.)

It would be better to avoid spawning subprocesses for this kind of simple queries whenever possible. Even the mapping query itself could be rewritten to just read a single line of `/proc/{pid}/status` without contacting the Docker daemon API at all.

I think we could rewrite `get_container_process_table()` as a follow-up PR.

Hi @AllanOXDi - lots of new work here, which is really exciting to see! There is some smoothing out to be done on a few parts of the interface, as well as with some of the CRUD functionality of the device syncing schedule itself. To start with the syncing, I've added an inline comment about editing, but also wanted to add a little bit more context. When I click on the edit button, I do see the page is created and loads, but there are some console errors and no contents of the page. I'm not sure if this is the step you were at, or if the goal was to have the "edit" feature fully working. If you want to have the edit feature fully working, it seems like there are two errors happening, one related to your Vuex, and one related to the endpoint returning a 404 error. I'd suggest just debugging one at a time (perhaps with some route adjustments to make sure the device ID is also getting passed, it seems like that might be part of the problem), and then seeing if you can bring both pieces together. As for the UI, there is some general work that can be done to help it match the figma even more closely, even though it's just small things. First up: - overall, the page text looks a little bit "crowded" here. You can look at the figma and perhaps add a little bit of spacing. In some places, if you are using just `{{ $tr('here's my string') }}` to add text inside of a `div` or a `span`, wrapping it in a `

` tag can add some default padding that makes it feel a little bit roomier. You may still need to add a `class` to that `

` and adjust the spacing, but that might be a place to start. - You can see that the contents shifts when the dropdown is opened, and that it's a bit hard to scroll. I think adding a default, fixed height to the main page container, rather than just having it the size of the content that is there (for example, making it always take up ~80% of the page) might be a good idea. What do you think @jtamiace? https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/17235236/219707498-b84f2035-002d-4e1f-87d3-4b5b8b381339.mp4 - Finally, this is a bit of a nitpick, but to save Jessica having to do it, the alignment on the left should all be in one line. One tool I use to help with "pixel perfect" UIs (well, they are pixel perfect in my mind, although Jessica and Tomiwa might have a different opinion) is a Chrome browser extension called "Grid Rule". If you use Chrome you can just search for it and add it on. Turn on the extension, and then you can "pull" lines from the top or the left, to check the alignment of various page elements. https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/17235236/219709118-11e1f1bc-d3f8-45d8-b63c-ee2fbb480348.mp4 On the main sync schedules page, I have two suggestions. - Add some kind of loading state. One option would be to have a loading state for the whole table. The other option would be to have a loading state just for the sync status, if the other pieces of the table (i.e. device name) wouldn't take an extra fetch. This would help reduce the time where it looks like there are no devices - I'm not sure why there are four statuses here. I tried to figure it out from the code and it seems like `myDevices` is being set to `tasks` - if there is one task per device, that would be right, but here, it seems like there might be more than one task per device, in which case we may need to rethink how this is being displayed to the user. https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/17235236/219710099-7028e4ea-5454-4242-bb06-d4133da2714e.mp4 Finally - and this maybe is a question for @rtibbles regarding the expected behavior (maybe with @bjester weighing in if any of the refactoring work you are doing could relate to this?) I am now running into fun edge cases since I'm on ZeroTier and I can see other's devices available. This morning I saw both Samson's and Allan's computers in the "devices" that were on network. Trying to set a sync schedule on these (logically) resulted in a 403 error. Do we want to filter those devices out entirely? Do we want to filter them out for now, but in the future, add an option to input admin credentials in them, so that they could sync? Just wanted to raise this so we can start thinking about it. Okay - that's all I have for now, but let me know any questions you have or where you might need more tips or details, and I will do my best to help! Great progress on this!

I agree that the `Compose` in the current state is not fully BC. However, `lambda`'s or local functions will not supported by the dataloader:

```py
import torch
from torch import nn
from torch.utils.data.graph import traverse
from torchdata.datapipes.iter import IterableWrapper


class Compose:
    def __init__(self, *transforms):
        self.transforms = transforms

    def __call__(self, input):
        for transform in self.transforms:
            input = transform(input)
        return input


class Foo(nn.Module):
    def forward(self, input):
        return input + 1


dataset = IterableWrapper([torch.tensor(1)]).map(
    Compose(
        Foo(),
        lambda input: input * 2,
    )
)

traverse(dataset)
```

```
_pickle.PicklingError: Can't pickle <function <lambda> at 0x7fabc90d2b90>: attribute lookup <lambda> on __main__ failed
```

IMO, we can go full BC now, but should deprecate the non `nn.Module` usage.

Sorry for the long delay in getting review done.
There are some minor changes needed, but we should also follow up on a couple of these scopes -- as noted below -- to make sure they are good to include.

---

The `<X>Scopes.<scope_name>` interface is pretty easy to use, and should be easy to document. It might be good to add a page to the docs for this, to ensure that these functions and constants are included. We can do that in a follow-up if you prefer.

One thing which is missing is that we need to decide on where these imports should be done from for users. Some options:
- `src/globus_sdk/__init__.py` --> `from globus_sdk import urn_scope_string`
- `src/globus_sdk/scopes.py` --> `from globus_sdk.scopes import urn_scope_string`

I think I prefer the latter, but both are fine.

> I didn't want to get clever with dynamically assigning attributes on the `XScopes` objects.

I think keeping it simple for a first cut is good, but I did raise the possibility of a `ScopeBuilder` or `ResourceServerScopes` object below. My reasoning centers more around dynamic cases, like GCS scopes, for which some kind of object to represent "the scopes for a resource server" might make sense as a model.

Another way to look at it is that if the interface for `AuthScopes` and `TransferScopes` is good, it may be nice to allow users to build a similar object dynamically, as in `MyGCSScopes = Foo(<id>)`.
